I need to find some heavy-duty sealant before the floodwaters get into the engine block.

I have to weld these support struts quickly or the whole garage roof is coming down.

I need to check the torque on these mounting bolts before the atmospheric pressure shifts from that anomaly.

I can hear a strange harmonic vibration in the engine block that matches the pulsing of that celestial rift.
If those plasma flares hit the hull, I will be scrubbing carbon scoring off the panels for a month.

If I step through that thing, I hope it doesn't strip the gears in my joints.
There is a serious misalignment in the energy ring that looks like a faulty weld.
I bet a bit of lubricant and a heavy-duty pry bar could open this thing wider.
I need to clear these vines before they snag on my exposed hydraulic lines.
My chassis is taking too many scrapes from these limestone walls and dense brush.
These humidity levels are risking a short circuit in my primary navigation sensor array.
I must calibrate my torque actuators to maintain grip on this unstable, muddy terrain.
The particulate matter from this jungle is clogging my cooling fans and overheating my core.
The silence is so heavy I can hear the rhythmic clicking of the cooling manifold.
I have checked the fuel lines three times now while waiting for the engine to turn.
This quiet is grating on my nerves while I wait for a sign of ignition.

I keep staring at the gauge, hoping for a needle flicker to break this dead air.

I don't care who you are, just get your hands off my specialized pneumatic torque wrench right now.
You're making a mistake threatening me while I'm holding a heavy-duty plasma cutter in my hand.
Keep shouting and I will make sure your transmission never shifts into gear again.

I keep this custom-built diagnostic scanner polished because it reads ECU faults faster than any factory tool.
My grandfather's heavy iron anvil is the only thing in the shop that doesn't budge during a hard strike.

I spent three years restoring this 1967 big-block engine until every single valve seated perfectly.
